coredata.dc:title: Multi-stimuli responsive mesoporous silica-coated carbon nanoparticles for chemo-photothermal therapy of tumor
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Hongyan
Zhao, Qinfu
Wang, Xiudan
Mao, Yuling
Chen, Caishun
Gao, Yikun
Sun, Changshan
Wang, Siling
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a traceable dual-porous mesoporous silica-coated mesoporous carbon nanocomposite (MCN@Si) with high drug loading capacity and high photothermal conversion efficiency (30.5 %) was successfully prepared. Based on the nanocomposite, a pH/redox/near infrared (NIR) multi-stimuli responsive drug delivery system was constructed to realize the accurate drug delivery, drug controlled release and chemo-photothermal synergistic antitumor therapy. MCN@Si was used as a vehicle to load doxorubicin (DOX) with a high drug loading efficacy of 48.2 % and a NIR absorbance agent for photothermal therapy and NIR thermal imaging. Carbon dots (CDs) with proper size were covalently attached to the surface of MCN@Si via disulfide bonds to block the mesopores, preventing DOX premature release from DOX/MCN@Si-CDs. Besides, CDs were served as fluorescent probe to prove the visualization potential of the drug delivery system. DOX was rapidly released at the condition of low pH and high GSH concentration due to the breakage of disulfide bonds and protonation of DOX. Moreover, the local hyperthermia generated by MCN@Si-CDs under NIR irradiation could not only directly kill cells, but also accelerate DOX release and enhance cells sensitivity and permeability. Two-dimensional cells and three-dimensional tumor spheroids assays illustrated that DOX/MCN@Si-CDs + NIR group exhibited a superior thermochemotherapy synergistic treatment effect and the combination index (CI) was 0.378. Biodistribution study showed the biosecurity of preparations and its prolonged detention time in tumor sites. Besides, antitumor experiment in vivo also performed the excellent synergistic inhibition effect. All the results demonstrated that DOX/MCN@Si-CDs is a traceable multi-stimuli responsive nanodelivery system and can achieve efficient chemo-photothermal synergistic antitumor therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mesoporous silica-coated mesoporous carbon
Stimuli-response
Controlled release
Carbon dots
Chemo-photothermal synergistic therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs, MCN-COOH and MCN@Si
Combination therapy in vivo and histological examination
Preparation and characterization of MCN-COOH and MCN@Si
In vitro photothermal properties of MCN-COOH and MCN@Si
Preparation and characterization of CDs and MCN@Si-CDs
Drug loading capacity, drug existing state and in vitro stimuli-responsive drug release
Cellular photothermal effect and cellular uptake studies
Therapy effect against multicellular tumor spheroids in vitro

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Construction procedures of (A) DOX/MCN@Si-CDs and (B) MCN@Si-CDs; (C) Schematic illustration of chemo-photothermal synergistic therapy against tumors.

Therapy plan of antitumor experiment in vivo (① Administration via tail vein injection (containing 5 mg/kg DOX) ; ② Irradiation tumor sites by 808 nm NIR laser of 1 W/cm2 for 3 min after administration 2 h).

TEM images of (A) MCN-COOH and (B) MCN@Si; (C) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms and pore size distribution curves of MCN-COOH and MCN@Si; (D) TEM image of MCN@Si (a), corresponding EDX mappings of C (b), O (c) and Si (d) elements and the merged image (e) of C, O and Si elements; (E) XPS patterns, (F) FT-IR spectra and (G) TGA curves of MCN-COOH and MCN@Si (inset: the photos of MCN-COOH and MCN@Si before and after calcination, and the corresponding content of silica after calcination).

NIR-induced heating profiles of (A) MCN-COOH and (B) MCN@Si suspensions with different concentrations; NIR-induced heating profiles of (C) MCN-COOH and (D) MCN@Si suspensions irradiated at different laser power densities; Photothermal stability of (E) MCN-COOH and (F) MCN@Si; (G) UV–vis-NIR scanning spectra (water as the blank, MSN as the control); Linear time data versus –lnθ obtained from the cooling stage of (H) MCN-COOH and (I) MCN@Si photothermal effect curves (Inset).

(A) TEM image of CDs; PL emission spectra of (B) CDs and (E) MCN@Si-CDs under different excitation wavelengths; UV–vis absorption spectra, excitation and emission spectra of (C) CDs and (F) MCN@Si-CDs; (D) Excitation and emission spectra of MCN-CDs (Ps: all illustrations are the photos of sample in the bright field and UV light at 360 nm).

(A) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms and (B) pore size distribution curves of MCN@Si, MCN@Si-SH and MCN@Si-CDs; (C) Size distribution of MCN@Si and MCN@Si-CDs; (D) Zeta potentials results (n = 3); (E) UV–vis absorption spectra of PBS, MPTMS and MCN@Si-SH supernatants after reacted with DTNB (inset: the photos of corresponding supernatants); (F) Fluorescence spectra of MCN@Si-CDs supernatant after adding 0, 1, 2, 3 and 6 mg GSH (inset: the photo of MCN@Si-CDs supernatants with or without 6 mg GSH under the UV light at 360 nm); (G) Images of MCN@Si and MCN@Si-CDs dispersed in PBS (pH 7.4) and water; (H) Photographs and (I) hemolysis percentage of MCN@Si and MCN@Si-CDs (n = 3).

(A) XRD and (D) DSC patterns; (B) Drug release profiles of DOX/MCN@Si-SS-COOH and DOX/MCN@Si-CDs in pH 7.4 and 5.0 PBS (n = 3); (C) Drug release profiles of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs with/without GSH in pH 7.4 and 5.0 PBS (n = 3); Drug release profiles of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs with GSH and NIR irradiation at 2 W/cm2 in (E) pH 7.4 and (F) pH 5.0 PBS (n = 3).

(A) CLSM images of 4T1 cells after incubation with MCN@Si-CDs, DOX and DOX/MCN@Si-CDs ± NIR; The viability of 4T1 cells after incubation with MCN@Si-CDs, DOX and DOX/MCN@Si-CDs (C) with or (B) without NIR laser irradiation (n = 5); (D) Optical images of 4T1 multicellular tumor spheroids treated with different formulations for 3 days.

(A) Fluorescence images of main organs and tumor after treatment for 2, 6 and 12 h with DOX and DOX/MCN@Si-CDs ± NIR; (B) Tumor volume change curves (n = 5, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01); (C) Relative tumor mass (n = 5); (D) Body weight change curves of mice (n = 5); (E) Images of excised tumors (n = 5); (F) Thermal imaging photographs of mice treated with saline and MCN@Si-CDs under NIR irradiation (1 W/cm2).
H&E staining photographs of heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney and tumor from different treatment groups.
IC50 values of MCN@Si-CDs, DOX and DOX/MCN@Si-CDs ± NIR groups.

head.ce:title: Multi-stimuli responsive mesoporous silica-coated carbon nanoparticles for chemo-photothermal therapy of tumor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, 110016, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a traceable dual-porous mesoporous silica-coated mesoporous carbon nanocomposite (MCN@Si) with high drug loading capacity and high photothermal conversion efficiency (30.5 %) was successfully prepared. Based on the nanocomposite, a pH/redox/near infrared (NIR) multi-stimuli responsive drug delivery system was constructed to realize the accurate drug delivery, drug controlled release and chemo-photothermal synergistic antitumor therapy. MCN@Si was used as a vehicle to load doxorubicin (DOX) with a high drug loading efficacy of 48.2 % and a NIR absorbance agent for photothermal therapy and NIR thermal imaging. Carbon dots (CDs) with proper size were covalently attached to the surface of MCN@Si via disulfide bonds to block the mesopores, preventing DOX premature release from DOX/MCN@Si-CDs. Besides, CDs were served as fluorescent probe to prove the visualization potential of the drug delivery system. DOX was rapidly released at the condition of low pH and high GSH concentration due to the breakage of disulfide bonds and protonation of DOX. Moreover, the local hyperthermia generated by MCN@Si-CDs under NIR irradiation could not only directly kill cells, but also accelerate DOX release and enhance cells sensitivity and permeability. Two-dimensional cells and three-dimensional tumor spheroids assays illustrated that DOX/MCN@Si-CDs + NIR group exhibited a superior thermochemotherapy synergistic treatment effect and the combination index (CI) was 0.378. Biodistribution study showed the biosecurity of preparations and its prolonged detention time in tumor sites. Besides, antitumor experiment in vivo also performed the excellent synergistic inhibition effect. All the results demonstrated that DOX/MCN@Si-CDs is a traceable multi-stimuli responsive nanodelivery system and can achieve efficient chemo-photothermal synergistic antitumor therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, chemotherapy remains one of the most common ways to treat cancer. However, the application of chemotherapy was impeded thanks to the side effects to normal tissues, inadequate dosage and multidrug resistance to tumor sites [1,2]. Photothermal therapy (PTT), as a promising cancer therapy method, has caused widespread attention due to the high lethality to tumors and harmlessness to human body [3,4]. Local hyperthermia generated by NIR resonant materials via converting light into heat can not only directly kill tumor cells and promote drug release, but also enhance the sensitivity of chemotherapy and cellular uptake of drugs by changing the permeability of cell membrane to improve treatment efficacy [5,6]. However, it has been reported that utilization of PTT alone has no obvious tumor ablating effects due to the inadequate penetration depth of light [7]. To satisfy the treatment demands that individual chemotherapy or PTT alone cannot meet, combination therapy has been exploited to overcome the embarrassment. Under such circumstances, multifunctional chemo-photothermal synergistic treatment nanosized drug delivery platforms have attracted universally focus owing to the advantages of increasing drug accumulation at tumor sites, improving the antitumor effect and minimizing the invasive damage to healthy tissues [8–10].

To better treat tumors, various approaches have been reported in recent years [11,12]. Mesoporous nanomaterials were widely employed in drug delivery due to their unique ascendancies, such as large specific surface area and pore volume, adjustable aperture and size and high drug loading [13]. In our previous work, we have constructed a visible nanoplatform by grafting carbon dots (CDPEI) on the hollow mesoporous carbon (HMC) nanoparticles to realize the aim of chemo-photothermal synergistic therapy against tumors. However, the fluorescence of the CDPEI is quenched when being attached to the HMC, while it is visible only when the CDPEI breaking away from HMC, it is not good for observing the entire drug delivery process. A strategy was proposed to solve the florescence quenching problem caused by mesoporous carbon nanoparticles (MCN) in vitro by coating polymers on MCN to shield the π-π stacking interactions between carriers and quantum dots, however, the payload of drug was reduced after coating of polymers [13]. Thus, it is necessary to further explore a new strategy to address the florescence quenching problem and remain the high drug loading simultaneously.

Inspired by the intrinsic biocompatibility [14] and easily modified surface property [15] of mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN) and its application in cancer chemo-photothermal combination therapy by encapsulating NIR absorbing materials (e.g., gold nanorods [16,17], grapheme nanosheets [1], Pd@Ag [18], copper chalcogenides [19] and carbon materials [8,20,21]). Integrating the merits of both mesoporous silica and carbon as drug delivery vehicles to fabricate a nanocomposite via coating mesoporous silica on mesoporous carbon (MCN@Si) was proposed [22]. We suppose that the florescence quenching problem resulted by MCN could be solved after coating silica layer and the payload of drug could increase. Besides, MCN@Si nanocomposite possesses some unique advantages as the drug carrier: (1) higher hydrophilicity, dispersity and biocompatibility, (2) higher drug loading or co-delivery due to the dual-pore structure, (3) easily functionalized surface, (4) being able to realize track, and (5) outstanding photothermal conversion ability. In light of the mentioned virtues, MCN@Si could be utilized to construct an intelligent nanoplatform for chemo-photothermal synergistic treatment of tumors.

Preventing premature drug release is the key to improve therapy efficacy and reduce system toxicity. Plenty of gatekeepers, such as polymers [23–25], metal materials [26,27], biomacromolecules [28], inorganic nanoparticles [29] and quantum dots (QDs) [30,31], have been conjugated or coated on inorganic carriers. Among them, carbon dots (CDs) as a gatekeeper to inhibit drug leakage have received great attention by virtue of their superior feature, for instance, appropriate and controllable diameter, good water solubility and biocompatibility [32]. In addition, CDs have been applied in bio-imaging due to their fabulous luminescent property and photo-stability [33–35]. Therefore, CDs could be used as a cap to block drug and a fluorescent probe to monitor the drug delivery and release process in vitro. Certainly, drug release at tumor sites is also essential for therapy. The glutathione (GSH) concentration of tumor cells cytoplasm is at least 4 times than that of normal cells which provides a design idea to make drug precisely release at tumor sites [36,37]. Thus, redox-sensitive disulfide bonds, which can be cleaved under high GSH concentration [38–40], were selected to link carriers and CDs to make sure drugs could be released at the tumor site.

Herein, we proposed to address the fluorescence quenching problem caused by MCN via wrapping mesoporous silica on MCN, and fabricated a novel dual-porous MCN@Si with high drug loading and excellent photothermal conversion ability as drug carrier. Antitumor drug DOX was selected as a model drug to be encapsulated in the channels of carriers. DOX could rapidly release from carriers after arriving in tumor sites since the protonation effect of DOX in low pH environment. Therefore, a rational redox-, NIR- and pH- stimuli-responsive controlled-release nanodelivey system (DOX/MCN@Si-CDs) with ability to monitor system delivery process in vitro was established for the chemo-photothermal synergistic treatment of tumors (Scheme 1 ). After the nanoparticles reach in tumor by EPR-mediated targeting effect and are internalized by tumor cells, disulfide bonds are quickly broken and then gatekeepers CDs fall off the surface of the carrier to allow drug release. Moreover, acid environment and NIR irradiation also help drug release to produce synergistic effects. All the results indicated that DOX/MCN@Si-CDs is a promising visible nanoplatform and possesses excellent chemo-photothermal therapy synergistic therapy in vitro and in vivo with low system toxicity.

1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98 %), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98 %), 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX, 98 %), glutathione (GSH, 98 %), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), polyethyleneimine (PEI), 3-mercaptopropyltrimethoxysilane (MPTMS, 95 %), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (98 %), 5, 5′-Dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) and 2, 2′-Dithiodipyridine (Py-SS-Py, 98 %) were provided by Aladdin Chemical Inc. (Shanghai, China). Trypsin-EDTA solution (0.25 % and penicillin-streptomycin were all supplied by GIBCO, Invitrogen Co. Carlsbad, USA. Fetal bovine serum FBS, Roswell Park Memorial Institute RPMI-1640, fluorescent Hoechst 33258 and 3-4, 5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide MTT were purchased from Dalian Meilun Biotech Com. Ltd. Dalian, China. All other chemical reagents were analytical grade and used without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs, MCN-COOH and MCN@Si
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were synthesized in the light of the previous literature [41]. The carboxylated MCN (MCN-COOH) were synthesized in line with the method of the previous report [42]. One third of the MCN-COOH obtained above were dispersed in the mixture of H2O (25 mL), ethanol (15 mL) and CTAB (75 mg). Subsequently, ammonia (28−30 wt %, 0.275 mL) was added. And then, 0.125 mL TEOS was added to the above system with continuously stirring for 6 h. After centrifugation and rinsing, the products (denoted as MCN@Si) were collected for standby.

The prepared MCN@Si were dispersed in 50 mL ethanol, and 0.15 mL MPTMS was added. Then, the mixture was stirred with refluxing at 70 °C for 12 h. The surfactant CTAB templates were removed by refluxing in ammonium nitrate ethanol solution. After washing, MCN@Si-SH were obtained. Subsequently, 0.2 g MCN@Si-SH were dispersed in 80 mL ethanol and reacted with 80 mg Py-SS-Py to obtain MCN@Si-SS-Py. Then, MCN@Si-SS-Py were redispersed in 80 mL ethanol and reacted with 114 μL 3-mercaptopropionic acid to obtain MCN@Si-SS-COOH.

20 mg MCN@Si-SS-COOH were mixed with DOX aqueous solution with stirring overnight. Obtained DOX/MCN@Si-SS-COOH were activated by EDC and NHS and capped by CDs (10 mL) with continuously stirring for 24 h, and then centrifuging and washing the mixture until the supernatant being clear to attain DOX/MCN@Si-CDs. Meanwhile, the drug loading efficiency was calculated by measuring the absorbance of the total eluents at 480 nm. The MCN@Si-CDs were prepared as described above without drug loading process. In addition, the MCN-CDs were synthesized according to the previous literature [41].

The morphology of carries was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F30, FEI, Eindhoven, Netherlands). Specific surface area and pore size were measured by surface area and pore size analyzer (V-Sorb 2800 P, Gold APP Instrument Corporation, Beijing, China). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were conducted on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) and a FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker IFS 55, Faellanden, Switzerland), respectively. The content of coated mesoporous silica was measured by thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA 550, TA Instruments, USA). Size and Zeta potential by a Particle Size Analyzer Nicomp 380 (Particle Sizing Systems, USA). The UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra were scanned on a UV-4802H spectrophotometer (UNIC, Shanghai, China) and a SpectraMax M5 multifunctional microplate reader (Molecular Devices, USA), respectively.

The photothermal heating ability of MCN-COOH and MCN@Si were assessed to explore the photothermal efficiency change of MCN-COOH after wrapped mesoporous silica layer. Specifically, various concentrations of samples were irradiated by 808 nm NIR laser of 2 W /cm2, and 100 μg/mL sample suspensions were irradiated with various power densities. Furthermore, irradiating samples with NIR laser (2 W/cm2) to a certain temperature, then cooling to initial temperature and repeating the process for several times to assess the photothermal stability. The specific calculation method of photothermal conversion efficiency (η) is displayed in supporting information.

To testify the drug delivery system could prevent premature leakage of DOX, the DOX-loaded MCN@Si-SS-COOH with or without CDs-gated were dispersed in 3 mL release media (pH 7.4 and 5.0 PBS) and shaken at 37 °C. At the specified time, taking out 1 mL release medium of each group to determine the amount of drug release. The pH/redox/NIR multi-stimuli responsive drug release behaviors of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs were further assessed at different pH values (pH 5.0 and 7.4 PBS), with/without GSH and NIR conditions. The specific method was mostly similar to the above processes. Specially, for the NIR irradiation groups, irradiating the samples for 3 min at 2 W/cm2 at the indicated time gaps before measurement.

4T1 cells cultured in RPMI-1640 medium containing FBS (10 %, v/v) and streptomycin-penicillin (1 %, v/v) were seeded in six-well plates at a density of 2 × 105 cells/well and cultured at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for 24 h, and then the cells were treated with MCN@Si-CDs at concentrations of 0, 25, 50 and 100 μg/mL and incubated. After rinsing, digesting and collecting, the cells were redispersed in 200 μL PBS and illuminated by NIR laser of 2 W/cm2 for 3 min.

The 4T1 cellular uptake of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs was assessed through confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). Briefly, the cells were seeded into 24-well plates and treated with DOX, MCN@Si-CDs and DOX/MCN@Si-CDs (both containing 5 μg/mL DOX) and cultured for 2 h. NIR group was illuminated with NIR laser (2 W/cm2) for 3 min. Finally, the cells were observed by CLSM after staining with Hoechst 33258.

4T1 cells were seeded in 96-well plates and treated with different concentrations of DOX, MCN@Si-CDs or DOX/MCN@Si-CDs for another 24 h incubation. NIR irradiation groups were illuminated by NIR laser of 2 W/cm2 for 3 min. Subsequently, the cell viabilities were measured according to the MTT method. In addition, the combination index (CI) was calculated to assess the synergistic therapeutic effect of the formulation in supporting information.

Multicellular tumor spheroids were used to further estimate the synergistic therapeutic effect of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs in vitro. 4T1 cells were seeded at a density of 3000 cells per well after the agarose solution (1 %, w/v) was put into the bottom of 96-well plates. The tumor spheroids were administrated with different formulations after forming, observed and recorded under the inverted microscope for several days.

BALB/c mice (18−20 g) were subcutaneously injected 4T1 cells in the right rear flank to establish the tumor model. The mice were divided into three groups randomly and injected with DOX and DOX/MCN@Si-CDs ± NIR by vein, respectively. At specific time (2, 6 and 12 h), gathering the tumors and main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) of mice, then determining the distributions of DOX in tissues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Combination therapy in vivo and histological examination
ce:section.ce:para: The tumor-bearing mice were divided into five groups and treated with normal saline (control), DOX solution (5 mg/kg), MCN@Si-CDs + NIR, DOX/MCN@Si-CDs, and DOX/MCN@Si-CDs + NIR, respectively. In NIR treatment groups, the tumor regions were exposed to NIR laser (1 W/cm2) for 3 min after administration 2 h, and the images of mice and the temperature of local tumor were recorded. The specific therapy plan is shown in Scheme 2 . Meanwhile, body weights and tumor volumes of mice were measured during the period of treatment. Finally, the main organs and tumors were collected for hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of MCN-COOH and MCN@Si
ce:section.ce:para: The MCN were prepared according to the previous study [42]. In order to obtain a carrier with hydrophilic and easily functionalized surface, the wet oxidation method was adopted to prepare MCN-COOH. The morphology of MCN-COOH was characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, MCN-COOH with an average diameter of 95 nm had a mesoporous structure and exhibited a uniform and monodispersed spherical morphology. To synthesize a vector with more easy surface modification, favorable biocompatibility, high drug loading capability and the ability of preventing fluorescence quenching, an ordered mesoporous silica layer was uniformly coated on MCN-COOH by the surfactant-assisted process in ammonia solution to obtain MCN@Si. Surfactant CTAB was firstly used to block the mesopore of MCN-COOH and form micelles on the surface of MCN-COOH by hydrophobic effect and hydrogen bonds or electrostatic interaction. Then, TEOS was combined with CTAB by electrostatic interaction. Finally, CTAB was removed to expose the pores of carriers. TEM was employed to testify the morphology and size of MCN@Si. As exhibited in Fig. 1B, MCN@Si also exhibited a uniform and monodispersed spherical morphology and had a larger size (average diameter of 118 nm) compared to MCN-COOH. EDX mapping assay was applied to account for the carbon-silica composite. The images of EDX mapping showed that C element was the skeleton of MCN@Si and Si element was mainly distributed in the periphery of MCN-COOH to form a ring structure, further indicating the successful synthesis of MCN@Si (Fig. 1D). The coating of mesoporous silica layer on MCN-COOH was further evidenced by XPS and FT-IR tests. As exhibited in Fig. 1E, the new peaks appeared at 150 eV and 101 eV in MCN@Si were attributed to Si 2s and Si 2p, and MCN@Si possessed more oxygen compared to MCN-COOH, ascribed to the presence of mesoporous silica layer. Meanwhile, as performed in Fig. 1 F, 1701 cm−1 bands represented the CO stretching vibration of carboxyl groups appeared in MCN-COOH. After coating mesoporous silica layer on MCN-COOH, 1701 cm−1 bands disappeared and bands at 470 cm-1, 798 cm−1 and 1089 cm-1 appeared, which were attributed to Si-O bend vibration, Si-O-Si symmetrical stretching vibration and anti-symmetric stretching vibration, separately. To further quantify the wrapped mesoporous silica layer, calcination and TGA assays were carried out. Considering that MCN@Si was prepared on the basis of MCN-COOH, therefore, we designed the experiments to obtain the amount of mesoporous silica by comparison the calcination and TGA data of MCN@Si and MCN-COOH. After calcination to 600 °C for 3 h, MCN@Si group left white mesoporous silica with a content of 34 %, while the MCN-COOH remained nothing (Fig. 1G). The TGA results indicated that the content of mesoporous silica in MCN@Si was about 32.4 %, which was similar to that in calcination test (Fig. 1G).

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption test was executed to measure the mesoporous structure parameters of MCN-COOH and MCN@Si, the specific values are shown in Table S1. The specific surface area and pore volume of MCN-COOH were 621.1 m2/g and 0.92 cm3/g, respectively, and the pore size distribution of MCN-COOH was centered at 3.8 nm. MCN@Si had a specific surface area of 764.1 m2/g and pore volume of 0.68 cm3/g, whereas the pore size distribution curve shown in Fig. 1C revealed that MCN@Si had an additional pore at 2.2 nm compared with MCN-COOH, attributed to the mesoporous silica coating. The appearance of additional pore size proved that MCN@Si possessed a dual-pore structure. Therefore, MCN@Si could realize a high drug loading capability owing to their dual-pore structure and large specific surface area. All results mentioned above demonstrated that a dual-porous MCN@Si with significant drug loading capacity were successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro photothermal properties of MCN-COOH and MCN@Si
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the photothermal conversion capability of MCN-COOH and MCN@Si, their UV–vis-NIR absorption was first investigated. As displayed in Fig. 2 G, compared with MSN, which were synthesized according to our previous work [38], both MCN-COOH and MCN@Si exhibited relatively strong NIR absorption, revealing the potential photothermal conversion ability of them. The time-temperature curves were recorded to evaluate the photothermal effect of carriers. As shown in Fig. 2A and C, the temperature of MCN-COOH suspensions increased markedly at the increasing concentrations or increasing power density, whereas there was no apparent temperature change in control group (distilled water), suggesting that the photothermal effect of MCN-COOH was concentration-dependent and power density-dependent. Meanwhile, the near-infrared imaging photographs displayed in Fig. S2-A demonstrated the outstanding IR thermal imaging property of MCN-COOH. Additionally, Fig. 2E displayed that no obvious disparity was observed in the process of temperature changes after repeating the operation of NIR irradiation and natural cooling for five times, illustrating an excellent photothermal stability of MCN-COOH. In order to further investigate the change of photothermal effect after coating mesoporous silica on the MCN-COOH, the same experiments were employed. According to the Fig. 2B, D, F and Fig. S2-B, the results indicated that MCN@Si had similar photothermal properties to MCN-COOH, and the thermal heating capacity of MNC@Si was weaker than that of MCN-COOH, which was resulted by mesoporous silica on the MCN-COOH. Moreover, calculating the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) to quantify the thermal heating capability of MCN-COOH and MCN@Si, and the photothermal conversion efficiencies were 31 % and 30.5 %, respectively, computed by the obtained data (Fig. 2H and I). The result was consistent with the conclusion of MCN@Si had lower thermal heating capacity. In a word, despite the influence of mesoporous silica, the photothermal heating ability of MCN@Si was slightly inferior to that of MCN-COOH, but its outstanding photothermal conversion capability and photothermal stability still supported it as a potential PTT carrier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CDs and MCN@Si-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were prepared using different molecular weight (Mw) PEI, and the quantum yield of CDs was calculated by reference method. The quantum yield of CDs was highest (20.7 %) when the molecular weight of PEI is 10000 (Fig. S1). Thus, Mw 10000 PEI was selected to prepare CDs in the following study. The size and morphology of CDs were studied via TEM technique. As shown in Fig. 3 A, CDs exhibited a uniform monodisperse spherical morphology with an average diameter of 4−5 nm. Therefore, CDs with a similar size to the carrier mesopores could act as the gatekeepers to refrain the premature leakage of drugs. The optical properties of CDs were further detected by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. As to the fluorescence feature of CDs, the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra showed that CDs could be used for fluorescence imaging in vitro at specific excitation wavelengths, such as 360 nm for producing blue fluorescence (Fig. 3B). As displayed in Fig. 3C, in the UV–vis absorption spectrum, CDs had absorption peaks at 240 nm and 350 nm, separately, and in the fluorescence spectra, the maximum emission and maximum excitation wavelengths of CDs were at 450 nm and 360 nm, respectively. The inserted picture in Fig. 3C manifested that CDs showed the bright blue light under UV light irradiation at 360 nm. The physical state of CDs was studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD) test (Fig. S3), a wide diffraction peak centered at 22°, indicating that CDs was noncrystalline structure.

MCN@Si can be easily modified with –SH owing to the mesoporous silica coating. And then CDs used as gatekeepers were conjugated on the MCN@Si through disulfide bonds to enhance the biocompatibility of the carrier, the synthetic route of MCN@Si-CDs is described in Scheme 1. The successful preparation of MCN@Si-CDs was proved by various methods. The isotherms and pore size distribution curves of MCN@Si, MCN@Si-SH and MCN@Si-CDs are displayed in Fig. 4 A and B, and the specific parameters are gathered in Table S2. The data illustrated that both the specific surface area, pore size and total pore volume of nanoparticles decreased gradually as the modification procedure increased, especially after grafting disulfide bonds and CDs, indicating CDs were grafted on MCN@Si. The modification of –SH on MCN@Si was further certified via color reaction and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. MPTMS and MCN@Si-SH reacted with DTNB, respectively. Compared with blank group (pH 7.4 PBS) and control group (MPTMS), the appearance of both yellow in supernatant and absorption peak at 412 nm in MCN@Si-SH group confirmed the presence of -SH on MCN@Si (Fig. 4E). Meanwhile, the hydrodynamic diameter of MCN@Si-CDs was slightly larger than that of MCN@Si (Fig. 4C), which was due to the grafting of CDs on MCN@Si. And the polydispersity index (PDI) of MCN@Si-CDs (0.056) was smaller than that of blank MCN@Si, hinting its good dispersity (Table S3). Moreover, the changes of Zeta potential offered evidence for each-step modification (Fig. 4D). As shown in Fig. 3E and F, The fluorescence spectra of MCN@Si-CDs suspension were similar to that of CDs, demonstrating CDs have been grafted on the surface of MCN@Si. And there were some difference in UV–vis absorption spectrum between the MCN@Si-CDs suspension and CDs because of MCN@Si. Furthermore, it can be seen from the images inserted in Fig. 3D and F that the MCN@Si-CDs suspension still showed blue light after irradiating by UV light at 360 nm, whereas the blue fluorescence of MCN-CDs suspension was quenched under the same conditions. Thus, it can be inferred that coating MCN-COOH with mesoporous silica could shield fluorescence quenching. These results indicated that MCN@Si-CDs were successfully prepared and CDs attached to MCN@Si could not merely serve as fluorescent markers and gatekeepers for blocking the drug release from the carrier channels, but also improve the dispersibility of MCN@Si.

The dispersion stability and hemocompatibility tests were applied to assess the biosecurity of MCN@Si-CDs nanoparticles. As to the dispersion stability assay, MCN@Si and MCN@Si-CDs were evenly dispersed in pH 7.4 PBS and distilled water, respectively. Fig. 4G exhibited that MCN@Si and MCN@Si-CDs nanoparticles were relatively stable in distilled water. MCN@Si-CDs could maintain stable for 12 h under simulated physiological conditions, while MCN@Si flocculated and precipitated at the same conditions. The dispersion stability of MCN@Si-CDs was notably promoted after the grafting of CDs on MCN@Si, and this phenomenon was caused by the electrostatic repulsion force and steric hindrance effect of CDs. As shown in Fig. 4H and I, there was no noticeable hemolysis phenomenon in MCN@Si-CDs group compared to the bare MCN@Si group, and accordingly, the hemolysis percentage of MCN@Si-CDs was lower than that of MCN@Si. These results revealed that grafting of CDs on the surface of MCN@Si could enhance the biocompatibility of MCN@Si-CDs for in vivo application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading capacity, drug existing state and in vitro stimuli-responsive drug release
ce:section.ce:para: The drug loading efficiency of MCN@Si-CDs was up to 48.2 % by measuring the absorbance of DOX in the supernatant, which was higher than that of mesoporous carbon nanocarriers [13]. MCN@Si-CDs had relatively high loading capacity was not only resulted from the large specific area, strong absorption capacity and specific mesoporous structure of the carrier but also attributed to the electrostatic attraction and π–π stacking interaction between the carrier and drug. And more importantly, the dual-pore structure of MCN@Si gifted it a considerable drug loading capacity compared to the carrier coated with polymer, while achieving the same goal of shielding fluorescence quenching. Additionally, the existing state of the drug in DOX/MCN@Si-CDs was verified by using XRD and DSC techniques. As displayed in Fig. 5 A and D, no obvious characteristic crystalline peaks or melting point endothermic peaks of raw DOX were observed in DOX/MCN@Si-CDs group, implying the DOX existed in the mesoporous channels of the carrier in a non-crystalline state, which was induced by the restriction effect of mesoporous structure. Meanwhile, the non-crystalline of DOX was beneficial to facilitate its release.

To further testify that CDs could act as gatekeepers to impede the premature leakage of drugs, DOX/MCN@Si-SS-COOH and DOX/MCN@Si-CDs were dispersed in pH 7.4 and 5.0 PBS to investigate the release behaviors of DOX. The cumulative release amounts of both DOX/MCN@Si-SS-COOH and DOX/MCN@Si-CDs in pH 5.0 PBS were higher than that in pH 7.4 PBS (Fig. 5B), which was attributed to that the electrostatic force and π-π stacking force between DOX and nanocarriers were weakened due to the protonation of DOX under acidic condition. Compared with DOX/MCN@Si-SS-COOH, DOX/MCN@Si-CDs performed a relatively low release amount, proving the gatekeeper effect of CDs to restrict DOX release from mesoporous channels. Apart from the function of blocking the drug in channels, the other effect of CDs was explored. The fluorescence spectra of MCN@Si-CDs supernatant containing different amounts of GSH are illustrated in Fig. 4F. The elevated gradually fluorescence intensity in supernatants as the increase of GSH content was resulted from the shedding of CDs from MCN@Si-CDs. The change of supernatant blue fluorescence signified that CDs could monitor the release process of drug.

The drug release test of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs was carried out to further investigate the redox-responsive release property. At the same pH (7.4 or 5.0) condition, the cumulative release percentage of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs was distinctly increased in the existence of 10 mM GSH (Fig. 5C). This upshot demonstrated the drug release of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs was in possession of redox sensitivity. Meanwhile, the result of Fig. 4F revealed the mechanism of redox-responsive drug release. CDs linked by disulfide bonds could be dissociated from the carrier when encountering GSH to expose the pores, thereby promoting the drug release.

Besides, given the heating performance of MCN@Si, the photothermal effect on drug release was explored. As shown in Fig. 5E and F, both in pH 7.4 and 5.0 PBS release conditions, the drug release curves under NIR irradiation exhibited a terraced expedited release tendency. The release rate was significantly increased at the irradiated points, which could be interpreted by the fact that the raised temperature could accelerate the molecular thermodynamic movement of the drug and decrease the interaction forces between the carrier and the drug to facilitate drug diffusion [42]. Additionally, the maximized cumulative release amount of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs was 50.3 % under the condition of pH 5.0 PBS, GSH and NIR irradiation, it were 1.52-fold and 2.23-fold compared to the release amount under the single condition of GSH (33 %) or NIR irradiation (22.6 %), implying that DOX/MCN@Si-CDs had pH/NIR/redox stimuli-responsive drug release properties. Consequently, DOX/MCN@Si-CDs drug delivery system could realize accurate drug release under the multiple stimulus from tumor microenvironment (acid and redox) and outer environment (NIR) to minimize the side effect and strengthen the antitumor therapy effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular photothermal effect and cellular uptake studies
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular photothermal effect of MCN@Si-CDs was investigated in Fig. S4. The temperature of cell suspensions was gradually elevated as the nanoparticles concentration increased, whereas no apparent temperature change in blank cells group, manifesting that treated cells possessed photothermal conversion ability. This was ascribed to the fact that MCN@Si-CDs nanoparticles could be taken in cells and the increase of uptake amount could improve the cellular photothermal effect. Additionally, the temperature of MCN@Si-CDs, higher than 42 °C, could directly kill tumor cells. The results substantiated that MCN@Si-CDs had superior photothermal conversion efficiency on cytological level and tremendous prospect in tumor treatment.

The cellular uptake of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs in 4T1 cells was studied by using CLSM technique in Fig. 6 A. Only blue fluorescence in the nucleus was observed in blank vector group as expected, which could achieve cell localization. And red fluorescence overlapped with blue fluorescence in DOX group, suggesting that DOX could enter the cell nuclei resulted from the high affinity of DOX with DNA. Cells treated with DOX/MCN@Si-CDs for 2 h performed the relatively weak red fluorescence in cytoplasm, which was because that even though DOX/MCN@Si-CDs nanoparticles could be internalized by the cells, the release of DOX was restrict by the mesoporous channels of carriers. Moreover, intense red fluorescence appeared in cytoplasm in DOX/MCN@Si-CDs group after NIR laser irradiation, confirming that the DOX release was accelerated by NIR laser. This phenomenon could be interpreted by that the hyperthermia could increase the cellular endocytosis via improving the penetrability and fluidity of cell membrane and accelerate DOX release through promoting molecular thermodynamic movement.

The cytotoxicity of the drug delivery system against 4T1 cells with or without NIR irradiation was estimated by MTT assay to examine the biocompatibility, safety and chemo-photothermal synergistic therapy efficiency of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs. The cell viability results are displayed in Fig. 6B (without NIR irradiation) and 6C (NIR irradiation), and the specific IC50 values of each group are shown in Table 1 . The high cell viability (>80 %) was found in each concentration of MCN@Si-CDs group without NIR irradiation, proving the biocompatibility and safety of blank carriers. For the chemotherapy group, the cell viability decreased as the DOX concentration increased under NIR irradiation condition or not, performing a negative concentration-dependent trend, and no significant differences in cytotoxicity and IC50 values were observed between the two groups, indicating that the toxicity of NIR laser to cells was negligible. Nevertheless, MCN@Si-CDs group exhibited a slightly concentration-dependent cell inhibition rate after irradiation by NIR laser, further suggesting that NIR laser killed partial tumor cells by heat generated by MCN@Si-CDs. Besides, DOX/MCN@Si-CDs group without NIR irradiation possessed slightly high cell viability and IC50 value (8.058 μg/mL) compared to DOX group, however, DOX/MCN@Si-CDs group under NIR illumination exhibited a low IC50 value (1.513 μg/mL), which was ascribed to the combination of heat effect and chemotherapy. Apart from this, the combination index (CI) was calculated to evaluate the synergistic efficiency of combination treatment. Different CI values represent different treatment effects: CI < 1 means synergism, CI = 1 demonstrates additive effects and CI > 1 indicates antagonism. The CI value of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs combination therapy group was 0.378, showing a prominent synergistic therapeutic effect in tumor treatment. The result of maximal cytotoxicity in combination therapy group might be caused by high temperature, which could not only directly ablate the cells, but also facilitate drug release and improve the sensibility of the cells against antitumor drugs. In conclusion, the combination therapy group performed a superior synergetic therapeutic effect compared to chemotherapy or PTT alone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapy effect against multicellular tumor spheroids in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro multicellular tumor spheroids were used to mimic in vivo solid tumor for studying the therapy effect of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs under laser irradiation to solid tumor. The morphology and size changes of tumor spheroids within three days were recorded by inverted microscope in Fig. 6D. Except for the control groups, the tumor spheroids of other treatment groups underwent different degrees of damage, especially in synergistic therapy group, almost all cells were killed after three-day treatment. These exciting findings not only evidenced the superior therapy effect of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs but also provided a basis for subsequent experiments in vivo.

The distribution characteristic of DOX in vivo was investigated by injecting DOX and DOX/MCN@Si-CDs containing equal amounts DOX (5 mg/kg) into tumor-bearing mice for evaluating the retention capability of drug delivery system in tumor sites. The distribution results of DOX over time are displayed in Fig. 7 A. Due to the short half-life and rapid clearance of DOX, as expected, DOX was mainly distributed in tumor sites for DOX group, the maximum fluorescence appeared at 2 h and the fluorescence intensity declined as the prolongation of time. In contrast, the fluorescence signals of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs group in tumor sites increased with time, attributed to that DOX was gradually released from nanocarriers and DOX/MCN@Si-CDs nanoparticles could prolong the retention time of DOX via reinforcing permeability and retention effect. Additionally, the effect of NIR laser on distribution was studied. Raised intratumoral temperature by 808 nm laser irradiation was beneficial to nanoparticles internalization and intracellular drug release. NIR group performed a stronger fluorescent in tumors than that in DOX/MCN@Si-CDs group, verifying that NIR laser could increase the accumulation of drugs at the tumor site. Concisely, DOX/MCN@Si-CDs combined with NIR irradiation could realize high efficiency and low toxicity in tumor treatment, which was derived from the in vivo distribution results.

Given the superior properties and exciting antitumor effect in vitro of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs, the antitumor effect in vivo was researched via using tumor-bearing mice. As shown in Fig. 7B, during the treatment, tumors in saline control group continued to grow rapidly, while tumor growth in other treatment groups was suppressed to varying degrees. Especially in DOX/MCN@Si-CDs + NIR group, the volume of tumors at 15 day was greatly smaller than pretherapy, showing the best therapy effect to tumors. Similarly, the image of tumors (Fig. 7E) and relative tumor mass histogram (Fig. 7C), which were obtained after 15-day treatment, also validated that the antitumor therapy effect of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs combination therapy group (smallest tumors and lightest weight) was better than that of any monotherapy. Moreover, the body weight change trend chart of mice showed no significant weight loss in experiment groups except for the DOX group (Fig. 7D), demonstrating the toxicity of DOX and the biosafety of the prepared DOX/MCN@Si-CDs. It also certified the feasibility of combination treatment program since the toxicity of DOX was reduced after being loaded into nanocarriers. The local tumor temperature change photographs of mice treated with MCN@Si-CDs or saline in Fig. 7F, which could verify the PTT effect in vivo. Compared with mild temperature change of the saline group, the temperature of tumor area in MCN@Si-CDs group could rapidly reach 42 °C or higher (enough to kill tumor cells) after irradiation to achieve the aim of PTT. Additionally, H&E staining technique was adopted to further assess the antitumor effect and mechanism of combination therapy group. Tumors H&E staining pictures showed that cells in DOX/MCN@Si-CDs + NIR group were obviously apoptotic or necrotic compared to the other groups (Fig. 8 ). In summary, all the results illustrated that DOX/MCN@Si-CDs with NIR therapy regimen possessed an exciting prospect in tumor therapy. Besides, the high drug loading capacity of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs reduced the administration dosage, enhancing the safety of the system.

Major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney) excised from experimental animals were stained with H&E for histological analysis to further explore the latent toxicity of treatment methods on main organs. Fig. 8 showed that no conspicuous pathological change was observed in all experiment groups apart from DOX group exhibiting apparent cardiotoxicity, further suggesting the biocompatibility and safety of the DOX/MCN@Si-CDs.

In a word, considering the merits of both mesoporous carbon and mesoporous silica, a novel mesoporous silica-coated mesoporous carbon nanocomposite (MCN@Si) was fabricated to build a drug delivery system (DOX/MCN@Si-CDs) with multi-stimuli responsive controlled release and thermal-chemotherapy for tumor treatment. Compared with MCN-COOH, MCN@Si possesses high loading capacity and high photothermal conversion capacity (η = 30.5 %) which could be used as an excellent drug carrier and photothermal therapy agent, and it will not cause fluorescent quenching. The fluorescent CDs with suitable size grafted on the surface of MCN@Si were not only served as gatekeepers to prevent the premature leakage of DOX, but also used to certify that MCN@Si could be marked to track the drug delivery and release processes simultaneously in vitro. The in vitro release results exhibited an intelligent drug delivery system with pH/redox/NIR stimuli-responsive release properties. And all the results in cytological and animal level illustrated the superior chemo-photothermal synergistic antitumor treatment efficiency of DOX/MCN@Si-CDs + NIR compared with individual treatment. The key improvement in this work is that solving the fluorescence quenching problem caused by pure carbon and retaining the high loading capacity simultaneously, realizing the accurate drug controlled-release and chemo-photothermal synergistic therapy. Such a nanoplatform with high efficiency and low toxicity exhibited a promising prospect in antitumor and provided a new strategy for tracking in vivo.
The authors declared that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China Nos. 81603058 and81473165).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2020.110941.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
